title: Thadayam
review: Thadayam is a crime thriller set in 1999 in the border villages between Tamil Nadu and Andhra Pradesh. The series follows SI Adhiyaman (Samuthirakani), a sharp but largely overlooked officer, and Inspector Lakshmi (Sshivada). Using the old-school policing methods of the 90s, they investigate a series of gruesome and ritualistic murders that shake the region. What makes Thadayam interesting is that it doesn’t follow the usual “whodunnit?” format. The perpetrators are revealed to us fairly early in the series. Instead, the focus shifts to the motivations behind the crimes, the history leading up to them, and how the investigators piece together the evidence to finally catch the killers. The show also explores the personal lives of the main characters, especially the pre-marriage scenes involving Inspector Lakshmi, which feels unnecessary and slightly dilute the tension of the investigation. Despite this, the series remains an engaging watch. The screenplay is decent, and the period setting feels fairly authentic to the late 1990s. It also touches on interesting themes such as cross-border police tensions and the pressures honest officers face from politically connected individuals. Overall, Thadayam is a solid crime thriller that focuses more on the “why” and “how” of crime rather than simply the “who.

title: Yellow
review:
Yellow is a quiet, underrated gem. The film follows Aathirai (Aadhi), a bank employee frustrated with the monotony of her desk job. She takes leave and sets off on a solo trip without informing anyone to reconnect with a childhood friend she hasn’t seen in years. What starts as a simple journey slowly turns into something more transformative.

Along the way, she meets strangers who become friends, pushes herself outside her comfort zone, and tries things she never would have before. The trip also helps her process and find closure after a difficult breakup.

What makes Yellow stand out is its simplicity. There are no unnecessary songs or dance sequences, and the story is allowed to breathe. The film doesn’t rely on big stars either, but the acting across the board is excellent and feels very natural.

The visuals are beautiful, capturing both the landscapes and the character's emotional journey. It’s the kind of film that feels honest and refreshing; proof that you don’t need spectacle to tell a meaningful story.
